coredata.dc:description: The development of white light-emitting LED (WLED) is now one of the important demands of present society due to its energy saving feature. However, in this work, an ingenious strategy has been employed to develop Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based and rare-earth material free luminescent duo (LD) for the generation of white light. A colloidal LD, consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and DCM dye (DCM@N-GQDs), is employed for demonstrating the FRET. The band-gap is engineered by nitrogen (N) doping in GQDs, and an energy transfer efficiency of ~30% for white light generation is attained. The widely tunable PL emission from blue to the red region has been obtained by changing the D-A ratio. Therefore, the present work has provided an alternate approach to widen the light emission band of a conventional laser dye, which is otherwise restricted within a limited region of the visible spectrum. FRET-based WLED (F-WLEDs) with colour rendering index of 70 and correlated colour temperature of 4690 K have also been fabricated. The F-WLED exhibits an emission overlapping of 56% with the solar spectrum (AM 1.5) in visible region, which is doubled in compared to that of a commercial WLED. The present report of rapid synthesis of highly luminescent N doped GQDs and the strategy used here for generation of FRET based colloidal DCM-GQDs luminescent duo may also be extended further with other suitable laser dyes for further widening as well as tuning the spectral range of light emissions of different commercially available laser dyes and GQDs for their different photonic applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Illustration of the route for synthesis of N-GQDs from vitamin-C by using solid phase pyrolysis technique and FRET based generation of white light.

(a) TEM image of the specimen QGFs and the HRTEM image of a single QGF (~26 nm) is shown in the inset. (b) Enlarged version of the selected rectangular area A (upper panel) with the surface plot of the zone I and II (lower panel). (c) HRTEM image collected from a large portion in N-GQD0.2 specimen and inset-B is the same for a single N-GQD0.2. (d) Surface plot of the zone III and IV (upper panel) and enlarged version of the selected portion in inset-B and the corresponding 2D FFT pattern (lower panel). (e) The HRTEM image of N-GQDs0.7; enlarged version and 2D FFT pattern of a single N-GQD0.7 (~4 nm). (f) Raman spectra of the specimen (i) QGF, (ii) N-GQD0.2, and (iii) N-GQD0.7.
Formation of N-GQDs from QGFs in the presence of EDA.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of synthesized specimens; under 365 nm UV radiation (top inset) and variation of energy PL emission center with excitation energy (bottom inset). (b) PL emission (λex = 375 nm) spectra of synthesized specimens; inset shows the variation of emission center of N-GQDs with excitation energy. (c) Time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectra (λex = 375 nm, λem = 460 nm) of N-GQDs0.2, N-GQDs0.7 and N-GQDs1.0, are collected in TCSPC mode. (d) FTIR spectra of (i) N-GQDs0.2, (ii) N-GQDs0.7 and (iii) N-GQDs1.0.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra DCM (1), PL emission spectra (λex = 400 nm) of DCM (2) and (3). (b) PL emission spectra (λex = 400 nm) of N-GQDs0.7, DCM, and DCM@N-GQDs0.7. (c) PL Emission spectra (λex = 400 nm) of DCM@N-GQDs0.7 with different concentrations of DCM. (d) ϕFRET (%) plotted with distance r (nm) in between N-GQDs0.7 and DCM in different concentrations. (e) Variation of CIE colour co-ordinates of FRET-based LD system with different concentration of DCM. (f) PLQY (%) and FRET efficiency ϕFRET (%) of different donor specimens, i.e., N-GQDs.

(a) TRPL spectra (λex = 375 nm, λem = 460 nm) of N-GQDs0.7 and DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD. (b) Strategy for designing of F-WLED using DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD as light emitter and n-UV LED as pumping source, inset: digital photograph of DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD in PVA matrix under UV radiation.

(a) EL Emission spectrum of the as-designed F-WLED is operated with a biasing current of 8.0 mA, inset: the digital photograph of the corresponding F-WLED. (b) 1931 CIE colour chromaticity co-ordinates of G-LED, D-LED, and F-WLED, inset: digital photographs of the (i) G-LED and (ii) D-LED. (c) Thermal stability of the light emission intensity of F-WLED, inset: stability of the light emission intensity of F-WLED when it is continuously operated for 10 h. (d) CIE colour coordinates of the NTSC standard and the calculated RGB of F-WLED; inset: digital photographs of the (i) F-WLED, (ii-iv) RGB colour filtered F-WLED and (v) digital photograph corresponding to dispersion of white light emitted by F-WLED into its individual colours by a glass prism. (e) Emission spectra of C-WLED (red), G-LED (pink), D-LED (green), F-WLED (blue) and the AM 1.5 solar spectrum (black); inset: values of ESM for C-WLED, G-LED, D-LED, and F-WLED, are calculated with respect to the AM 1.5 solar spectrum. (f) Characteristics of a commercial solar cell is illuminated by C-WLED and F-WLED.

head.ce:title: Forster resonance energy transfer assisted white light generation and luminescence tuning in a colloidal graphene quantum dot-dye system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: FRET assisted generation of white light in doped graphene quantum dot-DCM Dye luminescent duo (LD) system for fabrication of WLEDs.

The development of white light-emitting LED (WLED) is now one of the important demands of present society due to its energy saving feature. However, in this work, an ingenious strategy has been employed to develop Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based and rare-earth material free luminescent duo (LD) for the generation of white light. A colloidal LD, consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and DCM dye (DCM@N-GQDs), is employed for demonstrating the FRET. The band-gap is engineered by nitrogen (N) doping in GQDs, and an energy transfer efficiency of ~30% for white light generation is attained. The widely tunable PL emission from blue to the red region has been obtained by changing the D-A ratio. Therefore, the present work has provided an alternate approach to widen the light emission band of a conventional laser dye, which is otherwise restricted within a limited region of the visible spectrum. FRET-based WLED (F-WLEDs) with colour rendering index of 70 and correlated colour temperature of 4690 K have also been fabricated. The F-WLED exhibits an emission overlapping of 56% with the solar spectrum (AM 1.5) in visible region, which is doubled in compared to that of a commercial WLED. The present report of rapid synthesis of highly luminescent N doped GQDs and the strategy used here for generation of FRET based colloidal DCM-GQDs luminescent duo may also be extended further with other suitable laser dyes for further widening as well as tuning the spectral range of light emissions of different commercially available laser dyes and GQDs for their different photonic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: After the introduction of incandescence and fluorescent lighting, more than a century ago, the recent inventions on solid-state light (SSL) emitting sources has facilitated technologists to bring revolution in photonics for applications in daily life. Contemporarily, according to the statistics of the US Department of Energy (DOE) in 2012, light-emitting diodes (LEDs) or more specifically the white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) will remains as one of the key members in SSL family for next two decades (2010–2030) due to their low energy consumption [1]. Traditionally, WLEDs could be fabricated by (i) combining three individual monochromatic LED elements emitting red, green and blue colours, or (ii) by pumping two or multiple phosphors partly, or (iii) by mixing light emitted from blue, green, and red phosphorus, where near-UV LED (n-UV LED) chips are employed as pumping sources [2–4]. However, the process for generation of white light by using individual monochromatic LEDs and blue/UV excitable phosphor-based colour conversion materials are suffering from many drawbacks. The lowering in efficiency due to reabsorption, low luminescence efficiency, sedimentation, low thermal stability and high manufacturing cost are a few problems in use of multi-phased phosphor light conversion systems in WLEDs [4]. Additionally, rare-earth elements (REs), which are often used as multiple luminescent centers in single-phase phosphors, has poor thermal stability, toxicity, high cost, and limited resources [5]. Therefore, the euphoria of research has been grown on the synthesis of the RE free light conversion materials and their white light-emitting (WLE) applications [6,7]. Whereas, the use of non-radiative Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) in between a suitable donor-acceptor (D-A) pair is an emerging field of photon harvesting, and it could assist technologists in overcoming the shortcomings related to the conventional light conversion techniques during the fabrication of WLEDs. Moreover, it is possible to tune the characteristics of emitted light from WLEDs just by varying the D-A ratio in FRET based systems [8]. Li et al., has fabricated WLEDs by utilizing the FRET mechanism in organic fluorescent dyes [9]. However, the essential requirements to have efficient energy transfer are: (i) the donor species should have high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), (ii) acceptor species should reside in close proximity of donor species and most importantly, and (iii) a good spectral overlap (SO) in between the emission of donor and absorption of acceptor [10]. Therefore, choosing suitable D-A combination is essentially needed for achieving FRET assisted white light emission. Due to several advantageous photophysical properties, such as (i) high sensitivity to electron disturbance, (ii) wide range of light emission in the long visible wavelength region, (iii) high fluorescent yield, and (iv) high photostability, 4-(Dicyanomethylene)-2-methyl-6-(4-dimethylaminostyryl)-4H-pyran (DCM) is one of the well-studied fluorescent dyes [11]. Moreover, by utilizing donor–π–acceptor (D–π–A) structure, DCM can be used as donor and as well as an acceptor dye in fluorescence energy transfer systems [12,13]. In this work, DCM dye with an absorption band at ~468.5 nm has been used as an acceptor to design a FRET-based luminescent due (LD) for white light generation.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have generated increasing worldwide attention among the researchers in 21st century due to their superior photostability, high PLQY, exceptional productivity, and low cost [14–16]. In particular, it has been found in the literature that by doping N atoms, PL emission properties of GQDs can be nicely tailored and there are myriad of synthetic approaches, such as reflux, microwaves, and solvothermal heating for synthesis of N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) [14]. Generally, these conventional synthesis methods are time consuming and syntheses of samples are carried out in liquid phase. As a consequence, challenge remains in separation and purification of the synthesized GQDs and often intensive care is required. In this context, solid phase pyrolysis, which has been used for synthesis of N-GQDs in this work, has following advantages, such as (i) no need of any solvent and, (ii) the direct contact between the reactants can speed up the reaction process reducing the energy consumption of the entire synthesis process [17]. As a result the solid phase pyrolysis is more economical and efficient for the synthesis of the GQDs. Also, with the development of WLEDs, synthesis of heteroatom doped GQDs with multiple emission centers have rapidly emerged. In most of the earlier cases, the RGB colour mixing technique is used during the fabrication of WLEDs by using GQDs. However, use of multicolour carbon dots (CDs) for RGB colour mixing and CDs containing hybrid material for white light emitting applications have the difficulty in tuning the mixture ratio of multiple phosphors during device fabrication [18]. In this regard, manipulation of photoluminescence (PL) emission center covering visible region (~350 to 700 nm) and improving PLQY are key issues with regard to the use of GQDs as RE free light conversion material. Recently, Tian el al., [19] have synthesized full-color emitting CDs from citric acid and urea in different solvents for making UV pumped WLEDs. Doping of REs, such as europium (Eu) and terbium (Tb) in blue-luminescent GQDs could also produce white light [20,21]. However, to avoid the toxic effect [22] and costly nature of REs: (i) engineering over the surface of CDs and (ii) doping of heteroatoms into their sp2 skeleton for white light emission is still being researched [23]. Also, it could help in improvement in PLQY and tuning of PL emission center in GQDs. Incidentally, by using the combination of ammonium citrate and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid as coordinating precursors, Zhang et al., has demonstrated the synthesis of highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (CDs) for the fabrication of WLEDs [24]. However, ultrafast strategies for the synthesis of high-quality N-GQDs at atmospheric pressure and from an environment-friendly precursor have been rarely reported. To design WLEDs, one-step synthesis of direct white light-emitting carbon-based nanomaterial has been reported earlier [22,24]. Recently, Madhu et al., [23] have reported the synthesis of white light-emitting CDs through microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride). However, in most of the earlier cases, complex and time-consuming fabrication routes are required to fabricate WLEDs [25,26]. The utilization of GQDs as a donor in a FRET couple could be a fruitful way to avoid such a complicacy in generation of white light [2]. Additionally, the possibility of bandgap engineering in a donor species is also a desirable criterion for achieving a good spectral overlap in between D-A pair [27]. In this regard, change in direct bandgap of GQDs via doping of heteroatoms such as B and N [28,29] could be an additional advantage in terms of their application as a donor in FRET process.

However, in this work, ingeniously, we have employed the FRET mechanism to generate white light and fabrication of WLEDs without using REs elements has been reported. Here, for the first time a FRET-based WLE colloidal luminescent duo has been developed, which is consisting of GQDs as a donor, and an organic laser dye, i.e. DCM as acceptor. A rapid solid-phase pyrolysis of vitamin-C (Ascorbic acid) has been carried out to synthesize N-GQDs at relatively low temperature of 120 °C at 20 min and at ambient pressure, in presence of EDA as nitrogen dopant. The structural features, bandgap of N-GQDs and hence their PL emission characteristics have been engineered by altering the amount of EDA. The evolution of NH3 from EDA at high temperature works as a catalyst in breaking and thinning of large oxidized quantum graphene flakes (QGFs) into tiny N-GQDs. Thus, enhancement in PLQY of cyan-blue emissive N-GQD from ~1.3 to 42% is realized. As the doping of N-atoms serves as a key element in bandgap engineering of as synthesized GQDs, a reliable spectral overlapping (~1.74 × 1018 M−1 cm−1 nm4) with DCM dye has been achieved and as a result an energy transfer efficiency of ~30% in FRET has been achieved for generation of white light. The shortening in PL lifetime of blue emissive N-GQDs in close proximity of DCM dye has been obtained which confirmed the nonradiative energy transfer between them. Interestingly, the calculated PLQY and CIE colour coordinate of the white light emitting colloidal LD system are found to be as high as 77% and (0.32, 0.35), respectively. The values of PLQY and CIE for the as-designed FRET-based LD system is better than some of the previous reports. In addition, the LD has been dispersed into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a highly transparent polymer, for fabricating n-UV pumped FRET-based WLED (F-WLED). The thermally stable F-WLED with the CCT value of 4690 K, exhibits a FRET dominant mode of warm white light emission within 0.5–10 mA operating current. Additionally, here we have successfully demonstrated a basic experiment of Optics, namely Newton's prism experiment by using the designed F-WLED and the observed result reveals its white light emission character in a clearer manner. The calculated value of colour rendering index (CRI) for F-WLED is 71 and is within the specified range for commercial WLEDs (65–80) [30]. However, the F-WLED shows an improved emission overlapping within the visible range of AM 1.5 solar spectrum in compared to that of a commercial WLED. Therefore, the generated white light could be fruitfully used for the purpose of solar energy harvesting, as has been demonstrated in this work. We believe our work on FRET-based generation of white light by using N-GQDs and DCM as an LD will open a new door for developing emerging RE free light conversion materials for fabrication of WLEDs and other optoelectronic applications.

All chemicals used here were of analytical grade. Vitamin C (L-ascorbic acid, 99%) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%), used as a precursor for the synthesis of N-GQDs, were purchased from Merck. Organic laser dye 4-(Dicyanomethylene)-2-methyl-6-(4-dimethylaminostyryl)-4H-pyran (DCM, 98%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Methanol (MeOH) which was used for making DCM solution was purchased from Merck. Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, Merck) was used as a transparent encapsulant for fabricating WLEDs. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

The method of preparation of N-GQDs is described below briefly. N-GQDs have been prepared by a simple solid-phase pyrolysis method. 1.0 gm of Vitamin C and 0.5 ml of EDA (N-dopant) are taken together in a ceramic crucible, cladded completely with aluminum foil. After adequately mixed at room temperature, the resultant is heated up to 120 °C in a baking oven for 20 min under atmospheric pressure. After cooling down to the room temperature, 20 ml DI water has been added to the as collected crude product and stirred for 10 min to make a uniform-dispersion. The as-prepared dark brownish suspension is then purified by filtering with micropore filter paper. The filter cake containing the bulk aggregates (impurities) is discarded, and the filtered solution containing the synthesized material has been centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min for further segregation of larger-sized agglomerated particles. The liquid sample containing the as-synthesized N-GQDs has been diluted in DI water for further characterizations and applications. It is worth noting that, to improve the PL intensity of the synthesized specimens, the amount of EDA is varied from 0 to 1 ml. The specimen thus synthesized without the addition of EDA is symbolized as QGF. Whereas, those specimens which have been synthesized by using 0.2, 0.5, 0.7, and 1.0 ml of EDA are symbolized N-GQD0.2, N-GQD0.5, N-GQD0.7, and N-GQD1.0, respectively.

The F-WLED has been fabricated by a simple coating method, as reported in an earlier work of our research group [6]. Firstly, PVA hydrogel is prepared by dissolving 4 g of PVA in 40 ml DI water, and the mixture is heated at 90 °C with vigorous stirring for 30 min. Finally, the clear PVA hydrogel solution is cooled down to room temperature. After that, 3 ml of an aqueous mixture of N-GQDs and DCM (4% v/v) has been added with 3 ml of PVA hydrogel and kept for sonication for 30 min. Finally, for the fabrication of the F-WLED, the homogeneous PVA-sample hydrogel mixture has been coated on an n-UV LED chip (Probots Techno Solutions, Bangalore, India). The as-obtained coated LED is put into an oven at 100 °C for 60 min for curing. The same procedure has been followed to encapsulate the n-UV LED chip with N-GQDs and DCM separately, which are symbolized as D-LED and G-LED, respectively.

The morphological and structural characterization of the synthesized N-GQDs have been done by using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM 2100F) operated at accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The samples have been dropped on a copper grid coated with amorphous carbon and dried under ambient conditions for HRTEM analysis. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the synthesized sample has been performed by using the PHI 5000 Verse Probe II system. The micro-focussed (100 µm, 25 W, 15 KV) monochromatic Al-Kα source (hν = 1486.6 eV) with a hemispherical analyzer and a multichannel detector has been used during the XPS measurement. The microstructural characterization of the synthesized materials has been done with Raman spectroscopic measurement (ISA Lab Raman system) equipped with a 532 nm laser with a 50 objective, giving a spot size of ~1 mm with a spectral resolution better than 2 cm−1. Moreover, for optical characterization, the UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption characteristics of the as-synthesized N-GQDs have been recorded by using a double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (HITACHI U-3010) at room temperature. The PL emission characteristics of the samples have been measured at room temperature using a spectrofluorimeter (Perkin Elmer LS55) equipped with xenon flash lamp acts as a source of excitation. Time-resolved fluorescence lifetimes of the synthesized N-GQD specimens have been recorded in Horiba Delta Flex spectrometer with the help of time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) apparatus and picosecond diode Laser. In order to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of the synthesized sample Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic measurement has been carried out while taking the samples in liquid form by using an IR-spectrometer (NICOLET 6700). Emission spectra of WLEDs have been collected using an optical fiber spectrometer (AvaSpec-2048).

Fig. 2 depicts the microstructural data (TEM images) and Raman spectra of the synthesized specimens. The formation of QGFs of average size ~15 nm can be seen from the TEM image in Fig. 2a. The larger view of a single QGF can be seen in the HRTEM image in Fig. S1a. The synthesized QGF is found to be consisting with multiple layers of graphene, as confirmed by 2D FFT pattern and AFM image analysis as shown in Figs. S1c and d-e. However, the irregularity in the graphitic lattice of QGFs (zone A, inset of Fig. 2a) has been conclusively determined by using the surface plots (lower panel, Fig. 2b), which have been collected from the rectangular zones I and II (upper panel, Fig. 2b). Surface plot corresponding to zone I depict the irregularity in the graphitic lattice, and it may be due to the presence of oxidative species [31]. Whereas, the surface plot corresponding to zone II depicts the formation of relatively better graphitic lattice in QGFs. Additionally, a tendency of agglomeration has also been observed among the QGFs (Fig. S2a, Supporting Information). Such agglomeration in QGFs is often harmful in achieving high PL emission [32]. However, in this work, we have overcome such shortcomings via the addition of EDA with vitamin-C. The production of NH3 at high temperatures [33] caused cutting/erosion of QGFs into smaller N-GQDs via nucleophilic reaction with epoxy groups [34] as depicted schematically in Fig. 3.

Additionally, EDA caused confiscation of oxidative species over QGFs, and therefore recovery of defect-free graphitic lattice is made, as it has been observed for N-GQD0.2 in Fig. 2c. Moreover, surface plots corresponding to zone III and IV (Fig. 2c) have been used to show the (i) formation of N-GQDs0.2 via cutting and erosion of graphene layer and (ii) recovery of defect-free graphitic lattice. As can be seen in Fig. 1 d that the irregularity in the surface plot (in the top left) corresponding to zone III depicts the erosion of graphene layers in the presence of EDA to form N-GQDs0.2. Meanwhile, N-GQDs0.2 of size ~7 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.31 nm, corresponding to (1 0 0) plane of graphite [35], has also been showcased in Fig. 2
c (inset-B). Whereas, the recovery of the regular hexagonal graphitic lattice is clearly depicted through the (i) surface plot of zone IV (top right, Fig. 2d; (ii) enlarged HRTEM image and corresponding 2D FFT pattern in Fig. 2d-B. However, further addition of EDA with vitamin-C caused the formation of N-GQDs0.7 of average size ~4 nm, as can be seen from the HRTEM image in Fig. 2e, and the corresponding size distribution has been shown in Fig. S2b. The non-hexagonal lattice imperfections in N-GQDs0.7 has been arisen due to the insertion of N atoms [36,37], as can be easily seen from the enlarged HRTEM image and 2D Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern shown in the inset of Fig. 2e. Also, CN configuration within the sp2 graphitic lattice of N-GQDs has been determined from the XPS measurement, as shown in Fig. S3 (see Supporting Information for details). It is worthy to note that the density of pyridinic N (pyrrolic N) has been increased (decreased) in case of N-GQDs0.7 in compared to that of N-GQDs0.2. Also, the density of graphitic N is found to be increased with N doping concentration and becomes the highest (10%) for N-GQDs0.7.

The high density of pyridinic N and graphitic N in N-GQDs0.7 indicates the possibility of achieving higher radiative recombination in it, which has been confirmed by the respective PL emission measurement. But a further increase in the amount of N doping has caused the enhancement in the density of pyrrolic N in N-GQDs1.0 (Fig. S3d), and this has resulted in the quenching in PL intensity due to the formation of new nonradiative decay channels in N-GQDs1.0. Therefore, in the present study addition of EDA in reaction pot has led to structural modification of N-GQDs and hence the opening of new radiative decay channels as discussed later.

The extent of disorder or the degree of crystallinity of QGFs and N-GQDs has been investigated from the Raman spectral measurements, shown in Fig. 2f. It is found that there are two Raman active modes appearing at ~1375 cm−1 (d-band) and ~1530 cm−1 (D**-band), which are related to the defect states. However, downshift in position and band narrowing in G-band in N-GQDs0.7 (pG = 1625 cm−1, ΔωG = 137 cm−1) in compared to that of QGFs (pG = 1635 cm−1, ΔωG = 261 cm−1), may have been occurred due to the reduction of oxygen-related defects in the presence of EDA. A redshift of 25 cm−1 is observed in N-GQDs0.7 (~1625 cm−1) due to the doping of N-atoms in the sp2 core [38]. It has been found that the value of ID/IG is slightly decreased to 0.57 in N-GQDs0.2 due to the reduction of oxygen-related defects upon N doping. But on further increase in N doping, there is an increased formation of lattice defects in N-GQDs0.7 due to the doping of N atoms into the sp2 hybridized graphitic core. The presence of lattice defects in N-GQDs0.7 can be clearly seen from the HRTEM image and the corresponding 2D FFT pattern, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2e. The formation of such N doping induced sp3 lattice defect in N-GQDs0.7 has led to the enhancement in ID /IG to 0.90 in it from 0.57 of N-GQDs0.2. Such an interior decoration in the sp2 core of N-GQDs using N-atoms can suppress nonradiative transitions and helps in the formation of new radiative decay channels.

The linear optical properties of N-GQDs have been shown in Fig. 4 . As depicted by the UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 4a, QGFs, and N-GQDs0.2-1.0 are found to absorb radiations lying within 230–500 nm wavelength region, and it is due to π → π* transition in sp2 carbon core and n → π* transitions within the surface states [39].

The absorption peak at ~ 250 nm is appeared in as-synthesized QGFs due to π → π* transition in the CC bonds, and it has been red shifted to 275 nm in case of N-GQDs0.2. Such a redshift in the absorption band with the addition of N-dopant (EDA) has been occurred due to the existence of amino groups which act as an auxochrome [39]. However, weakening in the π → π* absorption band with the addition of N-dopant signifies the doping of N atoms in the sp2 core and formation of mid-gap energy states [40]. In compared to that of QGFs, N-GQDs not only exhibits much higher absorption intensity at 325 nm, corresponding to n → π* transition in CO bond, but also shows a broad absorption band along-with an absorption shoulder at ~375 nm, which has extended up-to 500 nm (Fig. 4a). The visible absorption band has occurred due to amino groups on the surface of N-GQDs [41]. However, the optimal visible absorption has been found to occur in N-GQDs0.7. The formation of N-atom induced mid gap energy states are found to be very much helpful in improving the PLQY in N-GQDs [30,42]. Whereas, the trap states due to amino groups on the surface are responsible for excitation dependent PL emission in N-GQDs [41], and it can be seen from the lower inset of Fig. 4 and S4a-e. On the other hand, the incorporation of N atoms into the sp 2 network of graphene has caused a reduction in π electron density due to the strong negative induction effect of N atoms [29], and it can contribute to band-gap opening. Such a band-gap opening in N-GQDs has been observed in our case when the EDA amount is increased (Fig. S5a, Supporting Information).

The PL emission intensity of N-GQDs0.7 has been enhanced by 25 times in compared to that of GQDs (Fig. 4b). The PLQY of N-GQDs0.7 has been calculated (see Supporting Information) in aqueous medium by using quinine sulfate as a reference, and it is found to be 42%, which is the highest among those of all other synthesized N-GQDs. It has been found that further addition of EDA in reaction system caused quenching in PL emission intensity of N-GQDs, which has been seen in case N-GQDs1.0. Fig. 4c depicts the PL decay curves of N-GQDs0.2, N-GQDs0.7, and N-GQDs1.0. The PL decay curves have been analyzed by fitting with a bi-exponential function:
(1)

where α1 and α2 are the constants specifying the weightage of lifetimes τ1 and τ2 . The average PL lifetime (τav ) can be evaluated by using the following equation:
(2)

The obtained values of α1 , α2 , τ1 , and τ2 are summarized in Table-S1. According to the literature [43–45], the short lifetime component (τ1 ) corresponds to emission from the intrinsic state, while the long lifetime component (τ2 ) is due to the emission from surface states which are present in N-GQDs. Enhancement in the value of τ1 from 1.58 to 1.71 ns has been occurred due to the formation of new radiative recombination sites in core states of N-GQDs. The values of τav are determined to be 4.55, 6.17, and 5.68 ns for N-GQDs0.2, N-GQDs0.7, and N-GQDs1.0, respectively. Again, it is well known that the PL intensity, I ∝ τav/τR [42], where τR is the radiative recombination time. As mentioned previously, the PL intensity of N-GQDs has been enhanced by varying the amount of EDA from 0 to 0.7 ml and consequently PL decay time (τav ) for N-GQDs0.7 is found to be 1.35 times higher than that of N-GQDs0.2. Also, by considering the above proportionality, τR for N-GQDs0.7 is found to be 0.4 times lower than that of N-GQDs0.2. Since a more efficient PL requires a shorter τR , these results indicate that the moderate use of EDA as an N-dopant is responsible for the formation of radiative recombination channels in N-GQDs and hence their PLQY has been boosted up. However, τav is reduced for N-GQDs1.0, as shown in Fig. S6, suggesting the reappearance of non-radiative decay channels, it may be due to the instability of chemisorbed N on C [46], since an excessive amount of EDA has been used for their synthesis. Moreover, the contribution from both Cπ*→π and Nπ*→π transitions are responsible for the observed blue emission in N-GQDs, as identified from the emission wavelength-dependent PL excitation (PLE) spectra and energy band diagram shown in Figs. S4f and S4g, respectively (Supporting Information). However, blue-shift in the position of PL emission center of N-GQDs with an increasing amount of nitrogen precursor has been observed (Fig. S5b) due to the bandgap opening. The presence of oxygen groups can acts as a nonradiative recombination center [39] in QGFs. Therefore, weak fluorescence has been observed in QGFs as well as in N-GQDs0.2.

As shown in Fig. 4d, the IR absorption bands which have occurred at (i) ~ 3441 cm−1 (due to νO-H, band 1) and (ii) ~ 1790–1590 cm−1 (due to νC=O in carboxylic acid and carbonyl moieties, band 2) in N-GQDs0.2 have become weaker in N-GQDs0.7. This observation suggests that the addition of EDA has caused a reduction of OH and CO groups due to amine functionalization [47], and hence the non-radiative recombination centers have been destroyed. Additionally, the amination and/or amidation (IR absorption band 3–4, Fig. 4d) can also create new radiative recombination centers that belonging to electron donating groups and it helps in improving the PLQY of N-GQDs through N-doping, as observed in this work. Meanwhile, the as-synthesized N-GQD specimen (N-GQDs0.7) also exhibits excellent photostability under 365 nm UV irradiation, in compared to that of commercial fluorescent dye quinine sulphate (see Fig. S7a in Supporting Information for details). In another experiment, only a 14% decrease in PL intensity of N-GQDs0.7 has been occurred (Fig. S7b) when it is heated at 70 °C for 60 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET assisted luminescence tuning in DCM dye: LD for white light generation
ce:section.ce:para:
The spectral position of PL emission in GQDs can be tuned by doping of heteroatoms [27,28], and it also helps in the optimization of spectral overlap with acceptor molecules. In this work, by doping N-atoms, the position of PL emission center, as well as value of PLQY of GQDs, have been tuned, and therefore as an emerging donor species, N-GQDs are obtained to establish FRET [48] mechanism in the presence of DCM as acceptor. Generally, a nonzero spectral overlap (SO) integral in between donor emission and acceptor absorption is required for FRET [49–51]. The explicit SO in between D-A pair can be realized from the normalized PL emission spectrum of the N-GQDs0.7 (λ ex = 400 nm) and the absorption spectrum of the DCM dye molecules shown in Fig. 5 a. However, the value of SO integral J(λ) for DCM@N-GQDs system has been calculated by using the following equation [52]:
(3)
J
( λ )
=
∫ 0 ∞
I D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )

d λ
∫ 0 ∞
I D
( λ )
d λ
where ID(λ) is the PL intensity of N-GQDs in the wavelength range of λ to λ + Δλ with the total intensity (area under the curve) normalized to unity. εΑ(λ) is the molar extinction coefficient of DCM at λ.

The estimated value of J(λ) for DCM@N-GQDs0.7 is found to be ~1.74 × 1018 M−1 cm−1 nm4. It could be seen from Table-S1 that the value of J(λ) is increased with the increase in the amount of N-dopant in GQDs and is due to the effect of doping induced band gap opening. Fig. 5b depicts the PL emission spectra of DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system along with the corresponding spectra of N-GQDs0.7 and DCM at similar concentrations to those found in the LD system. Additionally, the selected excitation wavelength for collecting the PL emission spectra is in the region of absorption minimum of the acceptor dye molecule, i.e., DCM (Fig. 5a), this observation ensures excitation of DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system via emission of N-GQDs0.7. As expected, the PL spectrum of the DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system is consisting of two bands, one at ~475 nm corresponding to N-GQDs0.7 and another at ~620 nm corresponding to DCM (Fig. 3b, orange spectra). Undeniably, in comparison to the PL emissions of N-GQDs0.7 and DCM dye (direct excitation) as depicted in Fig. 5b, an obvious decrement in the intensity of PL emission band of N-GQDs0.7 along with the enhancement of PL band of DCM dye have been observed for DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system. These observations exclude the probability of emission enhancement of DCM dye via direct excitation and give a clear signature of FRET mechanism. Moreover, the ET efficiency ϕET of the DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system can be estimated from the following equation: [51]

2 = 2/3 is the orientation factor, θD is the PLQY of donor, and η is the linear refractive index of the medium. Meanwhile, the value of R0 is calculated to be 10.5 nm for the DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system.

Fig. 5c depicts the PL spectra of the LD system when the concentration of DCM has been varied from 0 to 26 μM. By varying the concentration of DCM, the number of acceptors per donor (n) has been altered here. Thus, due to the energy transfer from N-GQDs0.7 to DCM [52], the intensity of the emission band related to N-GQDs0.7 (4% V/V) quenches with an increase in value of n. While the intensity of emission band related to DCM increased gradually under excitation at 400 nm. A similar kind of behavior in PL emission characteristic of the LD system has also been observed, when other species of N-GQDs have been used as a donor (Fig. S8a-d, Supporting Information). The values of ϕET have been estimated for different values of n, and therefore, the values of r have been calculated from Eq. (6) [51].

It can be seen from Fig. 5d that the value of ϕΕΤ for LD system depends on D-A separation r and hence on the concentration of DCM. Generally, ϕΕΤ can be expressed in terms of r as given in Eq. (7) [53]. Where, α = 4 for surface energy transfer (SET) and α = 6 for FRET.
(7)

In Fig. 5d, a comparison between the theoretical (Eq. (7), solid line) and experimental data (scatter points) has been shown. A good agreement between theory and experiment is observed using the value of R0 = 10.5 nm and α = 6. Therefore, in this work, the mechanism related to ET is of FRET type.

Also, light emission with different values CIE coordinates is achieved in the as-designed LD system by varying the concentration of DCM dye (Fig. 5e). However, at a certain concentration of DCM (10 μM) the DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD exhibits white light emission with PLQY and CIE coordinate of 77% and (0.35, 0.32), respectively. However, PLQY of other colours, which has been generated from the FRET-based DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD system, has been presented in Table S3 (Supporting Information). ET performance of the synthesized donor species for the generation of white light has been shown in Fig. 5f. The N-GQDs0.7 having PLQY of 42% exhibits superior ET performance with ϕΕΤ value of 30%, which is the highest among others. Also, the values of photometric parameters for the as-designed FRET-based LD system is comparable to or even higher than those WLE GQD-based nanomaterials, as reported earlier [15,19,54] (Table S4). Additionally, high-quality white-light illumination requires a source with CIE coordinates (0.33, 0.33), CCT in the range of 2500–6500 K, and CRI above 80 [55]. Therefore, the DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD is a promising while light emitting luminophore for the fabrication of WLEDs, as demonstrated in this work.

Also, to find the conjugation between N-GQDs0.7 and DCM, a systematic study on the comparative UV–vis absorption characteristics of DCM@N-GQDs0.7 system with those of N-GQDs0.7, and DCM dye has been carried out, and the results are shown in Fig. S9a. It has been observed that when DCM dye is combined with N-GQDs0.7, the absorption peak due to DCM dye is blue-shifted by ~15 nm, indicating the formation of some conjugation in between DCM and N-GQDs0.7. A similar shift of the absorbance peak has been reported previously in other systems, such as RhB dye conjugated with Ag nanoparticles [56] and carbon quantum dot conjugated with RhB dye [43] due to the formation of covalent bonding. Additionally, bonding between the D-A pair is strong enough, as it could be realized from the observed almost unaltered UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S9b) as collected for a different interval of time upon illumination in UV light. Moreover, the interactions between donor and acceptor pairs in FRET can be probed from PL lifetime measurement, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The tail fitting operation is suggesting that the decay curve of N-GQDs0.7 exhibits an extended lifetime component of 7.91 ns (36.46%) and a shorter one of 1.71 ns (65.50%). However, the lifetime measurement performed on the LD system reveals that the surface trapped states of N-GQDs0.7 are affected due to the presence of DCM dye (Table-S2, Supporting Information). Consequently, the average lifetime τav of N-GQDs0.7 has been decreased from 6.17 to 5.05 ns. The shortening in the PL lifetime of N-GQDs0.7 has confirmed the efficient ET between the D-A pair in our FRET-based colloidal LD system. Therefore, designing of RE free WLED has been envisioned (Fig. 6b) in this work, through fruitful utilization of white light emitted from the DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD. Thus the LD system has been encapsulated within transparent matrix PVA for practical applications. The PL emission spectra are shown in Fig. S8f depicts the FRET assisted tunable light emission behavior of LD in the PVA matrix.

In this work, the FRET assisted white light emission from DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD has been judiciously utilized for the fabrication of F-WLED by combining it with an n-UV LED chip (~390 nm) as shown in Fig. 6b (details in the experimental section). Additionally, we have also encapsulated the n-UV LED with N-GQDs0.7 and DCM dye individually to fabricate G-LED and D-LED, respectively. The electroluminescence (EL) spectrum, along with the digital photographs of the fabricated WLEDs are shown in Fig. 7 a-b, and Fig. S10a (Supporting Information).

The decrease in emission for N-GQDs and enhancement in the emission for DCM dye, in the case of F-WLED, signifies the occurrence of FRET mechanism during the emission of white light (Fig. S10a, Supporting Information). The typical white light emission can be realized from the EL spectrum shown in Fig. 7a, as it covers a broad spectral region from 380 to 700 nm of the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The words in the logo of NIT Durgapur are clearly visible when it is illuminated by F-WLED (Fig. 7a, inset). Meanwhile, the intensity of F-WLED is found to increase linearly with an increase in biasing current from 0.5 to 8.0 mA, and thereafter due to saturation in biasing current, the saturation in emission intensity has occurred (Fig. S10b). Such a linear enhancement in light intensity of F-WLED with biasing current is a consequence of almost entire absorption of excitation light from the n-UV LED chip and its conversion into white light. The basic strategy for the extraction of white light from F-WLED has been shown schematically in Fig. 6b. The mechanism for white light emission in the as-fabricated F-WLEDs can be explained as follows: (i) Initially after being excited by the n-UV LED chip (~390 nm), D type N-GQDs0.7 emits light in the wavelength region of ~475 nm, and due to the FRET process (ii) the A-type DCM dye molecules partially (30%) absorbs the cyan blue light-emitting photons having energy of ~2.61 eV (475 nm) and emits light in the wavelength region of ~620 nm. Therefore, FRET-based white light is obtained from F-WLED via an amalgamation of light emissions from N-GQDs0.7 and DCM dye. The calculated values of phototropic parameters, such as the CIE coordinates, luminous efficacy (LE), and correlated colour temperature (CCT) of optical radiation for all fabricated LEDs has been presented in Table-S5 (Supporting Information). The CIE colour chromaticity coordinates of G-LED and D-LED are estimated to be (0.17, 0.15) and (0.34, 0.21), respectively. However, due to FRET assisted emission of white light, the CIE colour chromaticity coordinate has been shifted to (0.35, 0.35), as shown in Fig. 7b. Also, the words in the logo of NIT Durgapur are poorly visible when it is illuminated by G-LED and D-LED (Fig. 7b, inset). The white light emitted from F-WLED results in a CRI of 71 with a colour CCT of 4690 K. The luminous efficacy of optical radiation (LER) of the generated white light also has been estimated by using following equation [57] and is found to be 209 lm W−1.
(8)
LER = 683
lm
W

P h

P ( λ ) d λ
where, Ph(λ) and P(λ) are the spectral distribution of the photopic curve and white light, respectively. 683 lm/W is the maximum LER for monochromatic light at 555 nm, the peak of the photopic curve.

The calculated value of LER is comparable to that of WLEDs using remote colour-converting InP/ZnS [58] and CdTe/2CdSe [59] quantum dots as the nanophosphors. Moreover, stability in the emission intensity of remote colour-converting nanophosphors based WLEDs is another important performance factor because n-UV LED chips can produce heat during its operation. Therefore, to verify the thermal stability characteristic of DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD in white light-emitting application, emission spectra of F-WLED have been collected at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 7c that at a temperature of 353 K (80 °C), the F-WLED can glow with a relative intensity of 91% in compared to that of its initial value. Additionally, the intensity of F-WLED is observed to be unaltered even after nonstop operation for 10 h, at a fixed biasing current of 8 mA. These observations suggest the thermal stability of F-WLED, where DCM@N-GQDs0.7 LD has been used as a remote colour-converting nanophosphor.

To explore the colour science in the generated white light for back light display applications, NTSC colour gamut coverage % for the F-WLED has been estimated (Fig. 7d). RGB spectral components in the as-produced white light have been filtrated by using commercially available blue (B), green (G), and red (R) colour filters (Fig. S10c). RGB colour components in the white light can be represented through (0.70, 0.30), (0.32, 0.60) and (0.16, 0.11), respectively, in the CIE colour coordinate space (green dots). The filtered RGB triangle is found to cover 70% (±2%) colour gamut of the NTSC colour space in CIE 1931 standard system. Moreover, dispersion of the light emitted from F-WLED by a glass prism (Fig. S10d, for the schematic of the experimental set-up) can be seen clearly by the digital photograph, as shown in of Fig. 7d (inset-v), and it justifiably indicates the presence of all fundamental wavelengths within the generated white light. As shown in Fig. 7e, in this work, we have also make a spectral comparison of a commercial WLED (C-WLED) and our F-WLED with the solar spectrum (AM 1.5). The emission spectrum of F-WLED is most compatible with the solar spectrum, with 56% of emission spectral matching (ESM). Therefore, as a proof of concept, a commercial solar cell (7 cm × 7 cm) has been driven under the illumination of F-WLED, and the current-voltage characteristics are shown in Fig. 7f. Due to high ESM value, the efficiency of solar cell is observed to be 3.5 times higher when it is operated under the illumination of F-WLED in compared to the C-WLED.

It has been found in the literature that by doping N atoms, PL emission properties of GQDs can be nicely tailored and there are myriad of synthetic approaches for synthesis of N-doped GQDs. However, the conventional synthesis methods are time consuming and there are challenges remain in separation and purification of the synthesized GQDs and often intensive care is required. In summary, unlike the previous study [15], herein we have demonstrated a rapid solid-phase pyrolysis of vitamin-C (Ascorbic acid) to synthesize highly luminescent N-GQDs at relatively low temperature of 120 °C at 20 min and at ambient pressure, in presence of EDA as the source of nitrogen dopant. The NH3 molecules, which have been formed due to thermal dissociation of EDA, have been acted as in-situ scissors and the undoped QGFs (~15 nm) have been cut into tiny N-GQDs (~4 nm) with a good PLQY of 42%. The band-gap engineering of N-GQDs has been achieved with the doping of N atoms into its sp2 graphitic network due to reduction of π electron density in presence of N atoms. Due to such a gradual opening of direct bandgap from 3.3 eV to 3.6 eV, a tunable PL emission is obtained in the as synthesized N-GQDs. Here, for the first time a FRET-based white light-emitting colloidal luminescent duo (LD) namely DCM@N-GQDs0.7 has been developed with an energy transfer efficiency of ~30%. Through a judicious choice of concentrations of N-atoms in N-GQDs0.7 and DCM dye concentrations a considerable value of spectral overlapping integral J(λ) of ~1.74 × 1018 M−1 cm−1 nm4 has been achieved for efficient generation of white light. The calculated values of PLQY and CIE colour coordinate of the white light emitting colloidal LD system are found out to be as high as 77% and (0.32, 0.35), respectively. The values of PLQY and CIE for the as-designed FRET-based LD system is better than some of the previous reports [16,20,54]. Finally, by using the white light emitting LD system in PVA encapsulant as a remote colour-converting medium, we have fabricated (proof-of-concept) a RE free F-WLED. The future application of the developed F-WLED in light science and technology is understandable from its CCT value of 4690 K, LER of 209 lm W−1, and CRI of 71. Additionally, here we have successfully demonstrated a basic experiment of Optics, namely Newton's prism experiment by using the designed F-WLED and the observed result reveals its white light emission character in a clearer manner. Also, due to a good ESM with AM 1.5 solar spectra, the F-WLED is shown to be capable of driving a commercial solar cell with an efficiency of 5.6%, which is 3.5 times better in terms of the performance of the commercially available WLED. Therefore, this investigation on the production of FRET-based white light and consequently, the designing of F-WLEDs as solid-state light-emitting sources could bring revolution in applications of GQDs in solar cell devices. The present report of rapid synthesis of highly luminescent N doped GQDs and the strategy used here for generation of FRET based colloidal GQDs-DCM luminescent duo may also be extended further with other suitable laser dyes for further widening as well as tuning the spectral range of light emissions of different commercially available laser dyes and GQDs for their different photonic applications.
